episode_title: Ivar Krustok: How Estonia’s Media Giant Builds AI That Actually Works
transcript_file: 2025-09-08 - Ivar Krustok How Estonia’s Media Giant Builds AI That Actually Works.txt
summary_claims: Estonia has the highest digital-only subscriber penetration rate in the world at around 21% of the population

Estonian, as a Finno-Ugric language on a completely separate language tree from most other languages, saw a disproportionately large improvement in AI language tool quality compared to other languages with the generative AI boom
The price of AI tokens has dropped to 'almost nothing' over the past one to two years, making previously expensive tools like cross-language translation economically viable at scale
AI-generated Estonian text has a detectable quality called 'Est English' where grammar is correct but sentence structure follows English patterns rather than native Estonian patterns

matches.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: around five to six articles have come out of it that otherwise probably would have been not noticed. Because again the volume is there, it's not the easiest that's a interface for a human being to actually go and find these cases and go through them.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Krustok details how Delfi automates translation across Baltic languages, transcription, summarization, and court case monitoring, freeing journalists from manual information gathering. The court case tool exemplifies quantified efficiency: it surfaces newsworthy cases from a volume impossible for humans to scan. Yet the human remains essential for deciding which cases merit deeper reporting and for crafting stories in authentic Estonian rather than the 'Est English' AI produces.
matches.matched_text: around five to six articles have come out of it that otherwise probably would have been not noticed. Because again the vi volume is there, it's not the easiest that's a interface for a human being to actually go and find these uh cases and go through them.

matches.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: we want to be upfront about what is happening to their data and how we're gonna use this data because I think Even moving further, this is sort of the barrier to AI generated news publishers and sort of trust driven AI using news publishers.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Krustok draws a clear line between 'AI generated news publishers' and 'trust driven AI using news publishers,' framing transparency about data handling as the differentiator. For the audience-facing image generation campaign, Delfi ensured no user images were stored or used for training, communicating this clearly. Estonia's comparatively high trust in news and high direct traffic insulate Delfi from platform dependency, but Krustok sees maintaining that trust through transparent AI use as essential.
matches.matched_text: we want to be upfront about what is happening to their data and how we're gonna use this data because I think uh Even moving further, this is sort of the barrier to AI generated uh news publishers and sort of trust driven AI using news publishers.

matches.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: We didn't want these pictures to be stored anywhere in our cloud infrastructure, we didn't want these images to go anywhere for training. All of these pictures, once you generated it, we didn't have this image, we didn't have the image uploaded.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Delfi enforces editorial boundaries around user-generated content and data handling. For their national costume image generation campaign, they ensured no images were retained or used for training. The internal ChatGPT-like platform is designed so editors can trust that uploaded files are not used for training and that deleted files are truly deleted. These represent operational ethics decisions embedded in technical architecture rather than formal written policies.
matches.matched_text: We didn't want these pictures to be stored anywhere in our cloud infrastructure, we didn't want these images to go anywhere for training. All of these pictures, once you generated it, we didn't have this image, we didn't have the image uploaded.

matches.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: we're trying to mix and match these different models to ensure that this sort of tool can handle a lot of things. that maybe if we were to provide it to all our editorial stuff, then that would mean that we would have to basically have let's say four or five different subscriptions.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: This is the episode's most deeply explored theme. Krustok details Delfi's entire technical stack: a Microsoft Azure/Fabric data infrastructure, vector databases for 25 years of archives across multiple languages, and a mix-and-match approach to model selection combining Azure-hosted models, Hugging Face open-source models, and specialized Chinese deep-research models. The build-vs-buy decision is nuanced: core IP and user data tools are built in-house, while commoditized features like summarization and polling use third-party vendors like Breach Media. RAG is central to the archival tool, and infrastructure scale challenges include optimizing for Estonian language and managing token costs across hundreds of employees.
matches.matched_text: we're trying to mix and match these different models to ensure that this sort of tool can handle a lot of things. that maybe if we were to provide it to all our editorial stuff, then that would mean that we would have to basically have let's say four or five different subscriptions.

matches.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: rather than maybe me starting from a point of teaching them, because we already have a good enough workforce that is good at using these tools. These are now split into these, let's say, gatherings. So more focused on the editorial side of course still in the company.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Krustok identifies literacy as one of Delfi's three AI buckets but admits it is the area he is least satisfied with. Initial efforts involved top-down training sessions and distributing 80+ premium ChatGPT licenses, but this approach proved insufficient. Delfi pivoted to biweekly open discussions where tech-savvy editorial staff share individual use cases and collectively identify which can scale company-wide. The goal shifted from general literacy to building specific tools first, then teaching around them, reflecting an experimentation culture that lets practice lead theory.
matches.matched_text: rather than maybe me starting from a point of teaching them, because we already have a a good enough workforce that uh is good at using these tools. These are now split into these, let's say, gatherings. So more focused on the editorial side of course still in the company.

matches.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: we currently try to put everything in three different buckets. So we have one that's sort of these everyday editorial tools. And then we have these experimental long term projects. And then the third bucket is everything related to the literacy inside the company and upskilling of workers
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Delfi's organizational approach is structured around Krustok's three-bucket framework: everyday editorial tools, experimental long-term projects, and internal literacy. As Chief AI and Innovation Officer with the data team reporting to him, Krustok describes cross-functional collaboration with a technical chief on the editorial side. Management gave him long runway to build foundational data infrastructure over years before it produced visible dashboards. The pilot-to-production pipeline is evident in how the court case tool evolved from an envisioned chat interface into push notifications, showing willingness to pivot based on actual use.
matches.matched_text: we currently try to put everything in three different buckets. So we have one that's sort of these everyday editorial teams. tools that a lot of uh your guests have already talked about. We can get into th later on. And then we have these uh experimental long term projects. So these would be in house sort of chat GPT like clones and these sort of things.

matches.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: if you're on a larger market, it's easy to get this sort of contracts where you get free licenses and so on and so on, you get better support. just because they value our news content, even if they're not saying it maybe as outly as I would like them to say it. But if you're on a smaller market then you have to sort of ensure that your four hundred, five hundred employees could also access these tools.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Krustok highlights the asymmetry between large and small markets in negotiating with Big Tech. Large English-language publishers get free licenses and better support because their content is valued, while small-market publishers like Delfi must self-fund access for hundreds of employees. Building their own ChatGPT-like platform is explicitly a counter-strategy to this dependency. The Microsoft Azure/Fabric partnership is leveraged strategically, but Krustok maintains flexibility by also using Hugging Face and Amazon infrastructure.
matches.matched_text: if you're on a larger market, it's easy to get this sort of contracts where you get free licenses and so on and so on, you get better support. just because they value our news content, even if they're not saying it maybe as outly as I I would like them to say it. But if you're on a smaller market then you have to sort of ensure that your four hundred, five hundred employees, uh because we have a large newsroom, that they could also access these tools.

matches.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: you wake up in the morning, when your work day starts, it will say that okay, yesterday You wrote these articles, you got these patriots, you got these subscriptions, it will give you a sort of summarization about your day.

matches.summary: Delfi is reimagining how data reaches journalists through personalized daily briefings via Microsoft Teams bots rather than traditional dashboards. The audience-facing national costume image generation campaign represents a new interactive format. The live cross-Baltic translation feed is a new product enabling editorial staff to monitor news across three countries in real time. Krustok envisions push-based data delivery replacing pull-based dashboard browsing, fundamentally changing how editorial staff interact with audience and performance data.
matches.matched_text: you wake up in the morning, uh when your work day starts, it will say that okay, yesterday You wrote uh these articles, you got these patriots, you got these subscriptions, it will give you a sort of summarization about your day.

matches.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: if you're losing X amount of page views, for instance that had, I don't know, let's say ten advertisements, five advertisements, depending on the device. And if now this user gets the same information from a small search box You're not gonna monetize it the same way.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Krustok articulates a sharp economic analysis of AI chat search products: they cannibalize page views and ad revenue without proven replacement revenue. Delfi's 21% digital subscriber penetration rate (claimed highest in the world) provides a strong revenue foundation. Cost reduction drives technical decisions, from token price drops enabling previously unaffordable tools to building in-house platforms to avoid per-seat licensing costs. The small-market dynamic forces creative cost management that larger publishers can avoid.
matches.matched_text: if you're losing X amount of page views, for instance that had, I don't know, let's say ten advertisements, five advertisements, depending on the device. And if now this user gets the same information from a small search box You're not gonna monetize it the same way.

The mention of ensuring editors know their data is not being trained on and that deleted files are truly deleted reflects internal governance norms rather than external regulatory compliance.

matches.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: you can get a working prototype in like three hours that's from zero with a design that and access with live data that's just working in three hours and usable.
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Krustok describes a detailed rapid prototyping workflow: MANUS AI for API documentation, Google Stitch for Figma designs, and Vercel's V0 for code generation, producing functional prototypes in as little as three hours. This pipeline bridges editorial and product by showing stakeholders working demos instead of presentations. Failure patterns are acknowledged openly: the archival chat tool requires users to prompt differently than ChatGPT, the image generation campaign revealed that Estonian faces were too serious for the smiling AI models, and the ad inventory bot took longer than expected because industry-specific terminology required extensive training.
matches.matched_text: you can get a working prototype in like three hours that's from zero with a design that and access with live data that's just working in three hours and usable.

matches.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: we can protect our data, but we don't know actually like how or when we'll reach some good conclusion with these tools
best_quote.speaker: Ivar Krustok

matches.summary: Data strategy is central to Delfi's entire AI approach. Krustok has spent eight years building the data infrastructure, migrating from Azure Synapse to Fabric, and constructing a foundation that makes adding new data sources easy. Privacy is a recurring concern: the internal ChatGPT clone ensures editorial data stays within Delfi's infrastructure, the image generation tool deliberately discards user photos after processing, and the choice to host models on own servers is partly motivated by data sovereignty. Finance data is kept in a separate, restricted pool while other data is made openly accessible internally.
matches.matched_text: we can protect our data, but we don't know actually like how or when we'll reach some good conclusion with these tools
